title: Go Intro
content:
Go Introduction

Designed for Scale

Go, also known as Golang, is an open-source programming language developed by Google. It was designed to be simple, efficient, and highly concurrent, making it ideal for modern cloud infrastructure and microservices.



  • Go is a statically typed, compiled language.
  • It features built-in support for concurrency via goroutines.
  • Its syntax is familiar to C but with improved memory safety and garbage collection.
  • Famous users include Google, Docker, Kubernetes, and Netflix.

title: Go Syntax
content:
Go Syntax

A Simple Hello World

Go syntax is clean and concise. Every Go file starts with a package declaration, followed by import statements, and then the code.

Hello World in Go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, CareerPath!")
}


Key Notes: No semicolons are required at the end of lines! The curly brace { must be on the same line as the statement.

title: Go Variables
content:
Go Variables

Declaring Data

In Go, there are several ways to declare variables. You can use the var keyword or the short declaration operator :=.

Variable Declaration
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    // Using var
    var name string = "Golang"
    
    // Short declaration (inside functions only)
    version := 1.21
    
    fmt.Println("Language:", name)
    fmt.Println("Version:", version)
}


  • Go is statically typed: Once a variable is declared as a type, it cannot change.
  • Zero values: Variables declared without a value get a default "zero value" (0 for int, "" for string).

title: Go Control Flow
content:
Go Control Flow

If, Else, and Loops

Go uses if, else, and only one looping construct: for. There is no while loop in Go, but for can behave like one!

Conditional & Loop Example
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    // Standard for loop
    for i := 1; i <= 3; i++ {
        fmt.Println("Iteration:", i)
    }

    // If statement
    score := 85
    if score >= 90 {
        fmt.Println("Excellent!")
    } else {
        fmt.Println("Good job!")
    }
}

title: Go Functions
content:
Go Functions

Reusable Blocks of Code

Functions are declared using the func keyword. Go functions can return multiple values!

Function Example
package main

import "fmt"

// A function that returns a sum
func add(a int, b int) int {
    return a + b
}

// A function that returns multiple values
func swap(x, y string) (string, string) {
    return y, x
}

func main() {
    result := add(10, 20)
    fmt.Println("Sum:", result)
    
    a, b := swap("hello", "world")
    fmt.Println("Swapped:", a, b)
}

title: Go Pointers
content:
Go Pointers

References to Values

Go has pointers, allowing you to pass references to values within your program. & generates a pointer, and * dereferences a pointer.

Pointers in Go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    i := 42
    p := &i // Point to i

    fmt.Println("Value at p:", *p) // Read i through p
    *p = 21 // Set i through p
    fmt.Println("New value of i:", i)
}

title: Arrays & Slices
content:
Go Arrays & Slices

Working with Collections

Arrays have a fixed size, while Slices are dynamic and much more common in Go.

Working with Slices
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    // A slice is dynamic
    nums := []int{1, 2, 3}
    nums = append(nums, 4, 5)
    
    fmt.Println("Slice:", nums)
    fmt.Println("Length:", len(nums))
}

title: Go Maps
content:
Go Maps

Key-Value Pairs

Maps associate keys with values. They are equivalent to dictionaries in Python or Objects in JS.

Map Example
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    // Create a map
    m := make(map[string]int)
    m["Apple"] = 5
    m["Banana"] = 10
    
    fmt.Println("Apple count:", m["Apple"])
    delete(m, "Apple")
}

title: Go Range
content:
Go Range

Iterating with Ease

The range keyword is used in for loops to iterate over slices, maps, strings, or channels.

Range Implementation
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    nums := []int{10, 20, 30}
    
    for index, value := range nums {
        fmt.Printf("Index: %d, Value: %d\n", index, value)
    }
}

title: Go Structs
content:
Go Structs

Custom Data Types

Go doesn't have classes in the traditional sense. Instead, it uses structs to group data together. You can also define methods on structs.

Struct & Method Example
package main

import "fmt"

type User struct {
    Username string
    Role     string
}

func (u User) Greeting() string {
    return "Hello, I am " + u.Username
}

func main() {
    me := User{Username: "GoDev", Role: "Backend"}
    fmt.Println(me.Greeting())
}

title: Go Interfaces
content:
Go Interfaces

Abstracting Behavior

Interfaces define a set of method signatures. A type implements an interface by implementing its methods — there is no explicit 'implements' keyword.

Interface Example
package main

import "fmt"

type Shape interface {
    Area() float64
}

// Any struct with Area() float64 implements Shape automatically

title: Go Concurrency
content:
Go Concurrency

Goroutines & Channels

Concurrency is Go's superpower. A Goroutine is a lightweight thread managed by the Go runtime. Channels are the way goroutines communicate with each other safely.

Goroutine Example
package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "time"
)

func sayHello() {
    fmt.Println("Hello from Goroutine!")
}

func main() {
    // Launch a goroutine
    go sayHello()
    
    fmt.Println("Main function continues...")
    time.Sleep(time.Millisecond * 10)
}


Adding the go keyword before a function call makes it run concurrently!

title: The Select Statement
content:
Go Select

Channel Orchestration

The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple communication operations. It's like a switch but for channels.

Select Example
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    c1 := make(chan string)
    
    select {
    case msg1 := <-c1:
        fmt.Println("Received", msg1)
    default:
        fmt.Println("No message yet")
    }
}

title: Error Handling
content:
Error Handling

Errors as Values

In Go, errors are explicit return values. This encourages developers to handle errors immediately where they occur.

The idiomatic Go check
package main

import (
    "errors"
    "fmt"
)

func risky() error {
    return errors.New("something went wrong")
}

func main() {
    if err := risky(); err != nil {
        fmt.Println("Caught error:", err)
    }
}

title: Packages & Modules
content:
Packages & Modules

Organizing Go Code

Every Go program is made of packages. A module is a collection of related Go packages that are released together.

Modular Go
# Initialize a new module
go mod init github.com/user/myproject

# Tidy dependencies
go mod tidy

title: Go Summary
content:
Go Course Summary 🚀

Mastering the Gopher Way 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the comprehensive Go curriculum. You've moved from basic syntax to advanced concurrency and project structure.

  • Foundations: Syntax, Variables, Control Flow, and Functions.
  • Collections: Slices, Arrays, Maps, and the Range keyword.
  • Architecture: Structs, Pointers, and implicit Interface implementation.
  • Backend Power: Concurrency with Goroutines, Channels, and Select.
  • Professional Go: Error Handling and Module management.

Go is the backbone of modern cloud-native software. Your next step: Build a REST API or a distributed systems tool!
